title: British Boxing Board of Control

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Governing body of professional boxing in the United Kingdom

British Boxing Board of Control

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Sport | [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") |
| Abbreviation | BBBofC |
| Founded | 1929[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Location | [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") |
| Replaced | [National Sporting Club](/wiki/National_Sporting_Club "National Sporting Club") |
| Official website | |
| [bbbofc.com](http://bbbofc.com/) | |
| [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") | |

The **British Boxing Board of Control** (**BBBofC**) is the governing body of [professional boxing](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Boxing_Board_of_Control&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The British Boxing Board of Control was formed in 1929 from the old [National Sporting Club](/wiki/National_Sporting_Club "National Sporting Club") and is headquartered in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").

Until 1948, it had a [colour bar](/wiki/Colour_bar "Colour bar") in effect by means of its Rule 24, which stated that title contestants "must have two white parents".[[3]](#cite_note-BBC2008-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The British Boxing Board of Control initially refused to grant [Jane Couch](/wiki/Jane_Couch "Jane Couch") a professional licence on the sole ground that she was a woman, and argued that [PMS](/wiki/Premenstrual_syndrome "Premenstrual syndrome") made women too unstable to box.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Claiming [sexual discrimination](/wiki/Sexual_discrimination "Sexual discrimination") and supported by the [Equal Opportunities Commission](/wiki/Equal_Opportunities_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Equal Opportunities Commission (United Kingdom)"), Couch managed to have this decision overturned by a [tribunal](/wiki/Tribunal "Tribunal") in March 1998.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The British Boxing Board of Control gives out the British Boxer of the Year award. [Natasha Jonas](/wiki/Natasha_Jonas "Natasha Jonas") became the first woman to win this award upon winning it for the year of 2022.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Later, in 2023, Jonas became the first black woman to receive a manager's license from the British Boxing Board of Control.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Boxing_Board_of_Control&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Councils")]

The Board divides the country into seven Area Councils: the Scottish Area, the Northern Ireland Area, the Welsh Area, the Northern Area, the Central Area (including the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man")), the Southern Area, and the Midlands Area.[[12]](#cite_note-12) There was previously a Western Area, which was merged with the Southern Area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Lonsdale Belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Boxing_Board_of_Control&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lonsdale Belt")]

Main article: [Lonsdale Belt](/wiki/Lonsdale_Belt "Lonsdale Belt")

The Board also sanctions bouts for British boxing's most prestigious title: the [Lonsdale Belt](/wiki/Lonsdale_Belt "Lonsdale Belt"). The Lonsdale Belt is awarded to the champion of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in each respective weight class and to win the belt outright it must be defended against a British challenger on at least three occasions.

## Scoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Boxing_Board_of_Control&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Scoring")]

The Board is known for its unique scoring system. Except for title fights (where the bout is scored by three judges, none of whom serve as fight referee), the referee is the sole scorer. After the bout (if the fight goes to [points decision](/wiki/Points_decision "Points decision")), the referee hands his decision to the MC and the winner is announced, the referee then raising the arm of the winner – or, in the event of a draw, both boxers' arms.
